factors.summary: The company has exposure to foreign exchange movements, particularly related to fuel costs and international operations.

evidence.text: Although we are currently able to obtain adequate supplies of aircraft fuel, we cannot predict the future availability, price volatility or cost of aircraft fuel. Natural disasters, political disruptions or wars involving oil-producing countries, changes in fuel-related governmental policy, the strength of the U.S. dollar against foreign currencies, changes in access to petroleum product pipelines and terminals, speculation in the energy futures markets, changes in aircraft fuel production capacity, environmental concerns and other unpredictable events may result in fuel supply shortages, additional fuel price volatility and cost increases in the future.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

sentiment.label: negative

factors.summary: There is a risk of labor-related disruptions due to potential strikes or other concerted refusals to work by employees.

evidence.text: In the case of a CBA that is amendable under the RLA, if no agreement is reached during direct negotiations between the parties, either party may request that the NMB appoint a federal mediator. The RLA prescribes no timetable for the direct negotiation and mediation processes, and it is not unusual for those processes to last for many months or even several years. If no agreement is reached in mediation, the NMB in its discretion may declare that an impasse exists and proffer binding arbitration to the parties. Either party may decline to submit to arbitration, and if arbitration is rejected by either party, a 30-day 'cooling off' period commences. During or after that period, a Presidential Emergency Board (PEB) may be established, which examines the parties' positions and recommends a solution. The PEB process lasts for 30 days and is followed by another 30-day 'cooling off' period. At the end of a 'cooling off' period, unless an agreement is reached or action is taken by Congress, the labor organization may exercise 'self-help,' such as a strike, which could materially adversely affect our business, results of operations and financial condition.
evidence.subsection: Union disputes, employee strikes and other labor-related disruptions may adversely affect our operations.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: There is a risk of labor-related disruptions due to potential strikes, which could materially adversely affect the company's operations.

factors.summary: Labor relations are a potential risk due to the possibility of strikes or other disruptions.

evidence.text: In the case of a CBA that is amendable under the RLA, if no agreement is reached during direct negotiations between the parties, either party may request that the NMB appoint a federal mediator. The RLA prescribes no timetable for the direct negotiation and mediation processes, and it is not unusual for those processes to last for many months or even several years. If no agreement is reached in mediation, the NMB in its discretion may declare that an impasse exists and proffer binding arbitration to the parties. Either party may decline to submit to arbitration, and if arbitration is rejected by either party, a 30-day 'cooling off' period commences. During or after that period, a Presidential Emergency Board (PEB) may be established, which examines the parties' positions and recommends a solution. The PEB process lasts for 30 days and is followed by another 30-day 'cooling off' period. At the end of a 'cooling off' period, unless an agreement is reached or action is taken by Congress, the labor organization may exercise 'self-help,' such as a strike, which could materially adversely affect our business, results of operations and financial condition.
evidence.subsection: Union disputes, employee strikes and other labor-related disruptions may adversely affect our operations.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Labor relations pose a risk due to the possibility of strikes or other disruptions that could impact business operations.

evidence.text: Our pension funding obligations are significant. The amount of these obligations will depend on the performance of investments held in trust by the pension plans, interest rates for determining liabilities and actuarial experience. Currently, our minimum funding obligation for our pension plans is subject to favorable temporary funding rules that are scheduled to expire at the end of 2017. Our minimum pension funding obligations are likely to increase materially beginning in 2019, when we will be required to make contributions relating to the 2018 fiscal year.

This increase in operating cash flows was offset in part by lower profitability in the first nine months of 2017 driven by higher wage rates and fuel costs, which were offset in part by higher revenues. Additionally, American made a $256 million supplemental contribution to its pension plans in the 2017 period.

evidence.text: We have recently instituted, and intend to institute in the future, changes to our business model to increase revenues and offset costs. factors.summary: The company is not currently hedging fuel costs, which leaves them exposed to fuel price fluctuations. This lack of hedging could negatively impact their financial performance if fuel prices rise.

evidence.text: As of September 30, 2017, we did not have any fuel hedging contracts outstanding. As such, and assuming we do not enter into any future transactions to hedge our fuel consumption, we will continue to be fully exposed to fluctuations in fuel prices.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: As of September 30, 2017, we did not have any fuel hedging contracts outstanding to hedge our fuel consumption. As such, and assuming we do not enter into any future transactions to hedge our fuel consumption, we will continue to be fully exposed to fluctuations in fuel prices.

factors.summary: Economic conditions present mixed influences on the business, acting as both a headwind and a tailwind. While a strong economy and demand environment have supported performance, challenges such as fuel costs, wage rates, competitive pricing, and external shocks like hurricanes have tempered these positive effects.

evidence.text: The economic environment is a headwind due to factors like fuel costs and wage rates affecting profitability.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: Despite a strong economy and demand environment, industry revenue results were mixed in the third quarter of 2017 in part due to competitive pricing actions, as well as the impact from hurricanes.
evidence.subsection: Financial Overview

factors.summary: The company is currently experiencing mixed trends in debt leverage. While it has shown a reduction in long-term debt and capital leases, indicating a deleveraging effort, its debt level remains higher than industry peers, and it maintains significant obligations, which could affect financial flexibility.

evidence.text: The company is deleveraging, with lower long-term debt and capital leases compared to previous periods.
evidence.subsection: Significant Indebtedness

evidence.text: As a result of the foregoing factors, we currently have a higher debt level and fewer unencumbered assets than our network airline peers.
evidence.subsection: Liquidity and Stockholder Returns

evidence.text: Our principal financing activities in the 2017 period included net proceeds of $2.2 billion from the issuance of debt, primarily the issuance of $1.3 billion of EETCs and $815 million borrowed in connection with the financing of certain aircraft. These cash inflows were offset in part by $1.8 billion in scheduled debt repayments, $1.4 billion in share repurchases and $150 million in dividend payments.
evidence.subsection: AAG

evidence.text: We have significant amounts of indebtedness and other obligations, including pension obligations, obligations to make future payments on flight equipment and property leases, and substantial non-cancelable obligations under aircraft and related spare engine purchase agreements. Moreover, currently a substantial portion of our assets are pledged to secure our indebtedness. Our substantial indebtedness and other obligations could have important consequences.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

factors.summary: Free cash flow is showing mixed trends across different subsections. While operating activities are improving, with a significant increase in net cash provided by operating activities, this is offset by decreases in other areas such as profitability and increased capital expenditures. Additionally, the company has positive free cash flow that supports both operations and shareholder returns, including substantial returns to stockholders and proceeds from aircraft transactions.

evidence.text: Operating Activities American’s net cash provided by operating activities was $2.7 billion and $1.8 billion for the first nine months of 2017 and 2016, respectively, a period-over-period increase of $969 million.
evidence.subsection: American

evidence.text: As of September 30, 2017, we had approximately $8.3 billion in total available liquidity, [...] We returned $411 million to our stockholders [...] and raised $500 million in net proceeds from aircraft sale-leaseback transactions.
evidence.subsection: Liquidity and Stockholder Returns

evidence.text: Our net cash provided by operating activities was $4.3 billion and $5.9 billion for the first nine months of 2017 and 2016, respectively, a period-over-period decrease of $1.6 billion. The decrease was primarily due to lower profitability in the first nine months of 2017 driven by higher wage rates and fuel costs, which were offset in part by higher revenues. Additionally, we made a $256 million supplemental contribution to our pension plans in the 2017 period.
evidence.subsection: AAG

factors.summary: The company is significantly increasing capital expenditures, primarily through the acquisition of numerous aircraft, indicating strong confidence in future growth. Both American and AAG subsections highlight substantial investments in property and equipment, including the purchase of 58 aircraft and a notable equity investment in China Southern Airlines. Additionally, the Liquidity and Stockholder Returns section emphasizes the use of historically low interest rates to finance new aircraft deliveries under a fleet renewal program, further underscoring the company's growth strategy.

evidence.text: American’s principal investing activities in the 2017 period included expenditures of $4.5 billion for property and equipment, primarily 58 aircraft, including 20 Airbus A321 aircraft, 15 Boeing 737-800 aircraft, 12 Embraer 175 aircraft, 10 Boeing 787 Family aircraft and one Boeing 737-8 MAX aircraft, as well as a $203 million equity investment in China Southern Airlines.
evidence.subsection: American

evidence.text: We continue to take advantage of historically low interest rates to finance new aircraft deliveries under our fleet renewal program. [...] We raised $500 million in net proceeds from aircraft sale-leaseback transactions.
evidence.subsection: Liquidity and Stockholder Returns

evidence.text: Our principal investing activities in the 2017 period included expenditures of $4.6 billion for property and equipment, primarily 58 aircraft, including 20 Airbus A321 aircraft, 15 Boeing 737-800 aircraft, 12 Embraer 175 aircraft, 10 Boeing 787 Family aircraft and one Boeing 737-8 MAX aircraft, as well as a $203 million equity investment in China Southern Airlines Company Limited (China Southern Airlines).
evidence.subsection: AAG

factors.summary: The company's market share appears to be under some pressure due to competition from low-cost carriers and potential industry consolidation. While the company is making strategic investments, such as a significant equity investment in China Southern Airlines, these efforts may not be sufficient to counteract the risks posed by low-cost carriers and industry changes.

evidence.text: American’s principal investing activities in the 2017 period included a $203 million equity investment in China Southern Airlines.
evidence.subsection: American

evidence.text: Low-cost carriers, including so-called ultra-low-cost carriers, have a profound impact on industry revenues. Using the advantage of low unit costs, these carriers offer lower fares in order to shift demand from larger, more established airlines, and represent significant competitors, particularly for customers who fly infrequently and are price sensitive and tend not to be loyal to any one particular carrier.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: The company faces pressure from low-cost carriers and industry consolidation, despite strategic investments.

factors.summary: The company is differentiating itself through operational improvements and customer service, as evidenced by being named the 2017 Airline of the Year. However, its competitive advantages, including a strong brand and customer loyalty program, are being challenged by market dynamics, particularly in the context of high fuel costs and volatility, which could negatively impact its operating results and liquidity.

evidence.text: The company is differentiating itself through operational improvements and customer service, as evidenced by being named the 2017 Airline of the Year by Air Transport World, which cited the integration work related to the Merger, our operational and customer service improvements and the investments we are making in our product.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: Our business is very dependent on the price and availability of aircraft fuel. Continued periods of high volatility in fuel costs, increased fuel prices or significant disruptions in the supply of aircraft fuel could have a significant negative impact on our operating results and liquidity.
evidence.subsection: Background

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: The company has strengths in operational improvements and customer service, but faces challenges from high fuel costs.

factors.summary: The competitive environment in the airline industry is highly intense and dynamic, with various factors contributing to its complexity. These include the presence of low-cost carriers, aggressive pricing actions by competitors, new entrants, and broader economic downturns that can decrease passenger demand and affect booking practices. Additionally, the industry faces competition from alternative transportation methods such as rail and private automobiles.

evidence.text: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic. Unfavorable conditions in broader economies have resulted, and may result in the future, in decreased passenger demand for air travel, changes in booking practices and related reactions by our competitors.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: Despite a strong economy and demand environment, industry revenue results were mixed in the third quarter of 2017 in part due to competitive pricing actions, as well as the impact from hurricanes.
evidence.subsection: Financial Overview

evidence.text: Our competitors include other major domestic airlines and foreign, regional and new entrant airlines, as well as joint ventures formed by some of these airlines, many of which have more financial or other resources and/or lower cost structures than ours, as well as other forms of transportation, including rail and private automobiles.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Intense competition, including from low-cost carriers and alternative transport, poses significant challenges.

factors.summary: The impact of interest rate changes on the company is mixed. While historically low interest rates have provided benefits such as lower borrowing costs and supported capital expenditures, there are risks associated with potential increases in borrowing costs due to credit rating downgrades, which could negatively affect net income and financial flexibility.

evidence.text: A decrease in our credit ratings could cause our borrowing costs to increase, which would increase our interest expense and could affect our net income, and our credit ratings could adversely affect our ability to obtain additional financing.
evidence.subsection: Credit Ratings

evidence.text: We continue to take advantage of historically low interest rates to finance new aircraft deliveries under our fleet renewal program. [...] We raised $500 million in net proceeds from aircraft sale-leaseback transactions.
evidence.subsection: Liquidity and Stockholder Returns

evidence.text: These obligations also impact our ability to obtain additional financing, if needed, and our flexibility in the conduct of our business, and could materially adversely affect our liquidity, results of operations and financial condition.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Low rates benefit borrowing costs, but risks exist from potential rate increases affecting net income.

factors.summary: The company is exposed to significant geopolitical risks, including instability in the Middle East, which has contributed to higher fuel prices and increased volatility. Geopolitical factors such as conflicts, terrorist attacks, and political disruptions can impact fuel supply, costs, and overall business performance.

evidence.text: Natural disasters, political disruptions or wars involving oil-producing countries, changes in fuel-related governmental policy, the strength of the U.S. dollar against foreign currencies, changes in access to petroleum product pipelines and terminals, speculation in the energy futures markets, changes in aircraft fuel production capacity, environmental concerns and other unpredictable events may result in fuel supply shortages, additional fuel price volatility and cost increases in the future.
evidence.subsection: Downturns in economic conditions could adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: Brent crude oil prices were higher in the 2017 period due to [...] geopolitical instability in the Middle East.
evidence.subsection: Financial Overview

evidence.text: Acts of terrorism or fear of such attacks, including elevated national threat warnings, wars or other military conflicts, may depress air travel, particularly on international routes, and cause declines in revenues and increases in costs.
evidence.subsection: We may be adversely affected by conflicts overseas or terrorist attacks; the travel industry continues to face ongoing security concerns.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Geopolitical instability increases fuel prices and volatility, impacting business performance.

factors.summary: The company faces significant environmental and noise regulations, including those related to climate change and greenhouse gas emissions, which are creating substantial compliance costs. However, the company does not consider environmental regulations to be a significant operational constraint.

evidence.text: The text does not explicitly mention environmental regulations or emissions requirements creating significant compliance costs or operational constraints.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is heavily taxed.

evidence.text: We are subject to increasingly stringent federal, state, local and foreign laws, regulations and ordinances relating to the protection of the environment and noise reduction...
evidence.subsection: We are subject to many forms of environmental and noise regulation and may incur substantial costs as a result.

evidence.text: There is increasing global regulatory focus on climate change and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. For example, in October 2016, International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) passed a resolution adopting the ICAO Carbon Offsetting and Reduction Scheme for International Aviation (CORSIA)...
evidence.subsection: We are subject to risks associated with climate change, including increased regulation to reduce emissions of greenhouse gases.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Significant compliance costs exist, but they are not seen as operational constraints.

factors.summary: The company's capacity utilization appears to be stable with slight increases in available seat miles, suggesting cautious expansion. However, the significant investment in new aircraft indicates a longer-term confidence in future demand.

evidence.text: American’s principal investing activities in the 2017 period included expenditures of $4.5 billion for property and equipment, primarily 58 aircraft, including 20 Airbus A321 aircraft, 15 Boeing 737-800 aircraft, 12 Embraer 175 aircraft, 10 Boeing 787 Family aircraft and one Boeing 737-8 MAX aircraft, as well as a $203 million equity investment in China Southern Airlines.
evidence.subsection: American

evidence.text: Available seat miles (millions) (b) 64,582 63,751 1.3% 184,665 183,985 0.4%
evidence.subsection: Operating Statistics

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Stable capacity utilization with investments in new aircraft indicating confidence in future demand.

factors.summary: Management's confidence appears mixed. While they are taking advantage of low interest rates to finance aircraft deliveries, indicating cautious optimism, the significant share repurchases and dividend payments suggest confidence in liquidity. However, concerns about high fuel costs and ongoing cost management efforts hint at underlying caution.

evidence.text: We continue to take advantage of historically low interest rates to finance new aircraft deliveries under our fleet renewal program. [...] We were well above that minimum level at September 30, 2017.
evidence.subsection: Liquidity and Stockholder Returns

evidence.text: Since July 2014, our Board of Directors has approved six share repurchase programs aggregating $11.0 billion of authority. As of September 30, 2017 , $677 million remained unused under a repurchase program that expires on December 31, 2018. During the three months ended September 30, 2017 , we repurchased 7.7 million shares of AAG common stock for $362 million. Our Board of Directors declared cash dividends totaling $150 million during the first nine months of 2017.
evidence.subsection: Share Repurchase Programs

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Mixed signals from management regarding financing and liquidity, with cautious optimism.

factors.summary: The company is heavily reliant on technology and automated systems for its operations, which are critical to its business. However, integrating these technology systems presents significant challenges that could impact operational efficiency and lead to delays.

evidence.text: We are highly dependent on existing and emerging technology and automated systems to operate our business. These technologies and systems include our computerized airline reservation system, flight operations systems, financial planning, management and accounting systems, telecommunications systems, website, maintenance systems and check-in kiosks.
evidence.subsection: We rely heavily on technology and automated systems to operate our business, and any failure of these technologies or systems could harm our business, results of operations and financial condition.

evidence.text: Among the principal risks of integrating our businesses and operations are the risks relating to integrating various computer, communications and other technology systems that will be necessary to operate US Airways and American as a single airline...
evidence.subsection: We face challenges in integrating our computer, communications and other technology systems.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: The company faces significant challenges in integrating technology systems, which could hinder operational efficiency.

factors.summary: The near-term risks are multifaceted, primarily involving financial and operational challenges. These include volatility in fuel prices, rising costs, labor shortages, and increased insurance expenses. Geopolitical instability and data security concerns further exacerbate these risks, potentially impacting the company's margins, operations, and reputation.

evidence.text: Brent crude oil prices were higher in the 2017 period due to reductions of global inventories driven in part by continued production restraint, as well as hurricane-related refinery disruptions and, more recently, to geopolitical instability in the Middle East.
evidence.subsection: Financial Overview

evidence.text: Mainline operating expenses increased $363 million, or 4.8%, in the third quarter of 2017 from the 2016 period. The increase in operating expenses was primarily driven by higher wage rates and fuel costs. Aircraft fuel and related taxes increased 12.7% primarily due to a 13.4% increase in the average price per gallon of fuel to $1.66 in the third quarter of 2017 from $1.46 in the 2016 period.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended September 30, 2017 Compared to Three Months Ended September 30, 2016

evidence.text: A higher than normal number of pilot retirements, more stringent duty time regulations, increased flight hour requirements for commercial airline pilots and other factors have caused a shortage of pilots which could materially adversely affect our business.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is intensely competitive and dynamic.

evidence.text: The terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001 led to a significant increase in insurance premiums and a decrease in the insurance coverage available to commercial air carriers. Accordingly, our insurance costs increased significantly, and our ability to continue to obtain insurance even at current prices remains uncertain.
evidence.subsection: We depend on a limited number of suppliers for aircraft, aircraft engines and parts.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Multiple serious risks including financial, operational, and external factors threaten the company's margins and stability.

factors.summary: Shareholder returns are being actively returned through a combination of dividend payments and share repurchases, signaling management confidence. The company has consistently demonstrated commitment to returning value to stockholders, as evidenced by regular dividend increases and significant share repurchases.

evidence.text: During the third quarter of 2017, we returned $411 million to our stockholders, including quarterly dividend payments of $49 million and the repurchase of $362 million of common stock, or 7.7 million shares.
evidence.subsection: Liquidity and Stockholder Returns

evidence.text: In October 2017, our Board of Directors declared a $0.10 per share dividend for stockholders of record on November 13, 2017, and payable on November 27, 2017.
evidence.subsection: Share Repurchase Programs

evidence.text: During the three months ended September 30, 2017, we repurchased 7.7 million shares of AAG common stock for $362 million at a weighted average cost per share of $46.97.
evidence.subsection: Share Repurchase Programs

evidence.text: In October 2017, we announced that our Board of Directors had declared a $0.10 per share dividend for stockholders of record on November 13, 2017, and payable on November 27, 2017.
evidence.subsection: Share Repurchase Programs

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: The company is actively returning value to shareholders through consistent dividends and share repurchases, indicating confidence.

factors.summary: The company's near-term results are significantly influenced by fuel costs, economic conditions, and global events impacting travel behavior. Additionally, interest rates and disease outbreaks also play a role in affecting financial performance and operations.

evidence.text: The U.S. Airline Industry [...] With respect to fuel costs, the price of Brent crude oil per barrel, which jet fuel prices tend to follow, was on average approximately 13% higher in the third quarter of 2017 as compared to the 2016 period. [...] The economic environment remains a significant factor with continued periods of high volatility in fuel costs and global events affecting travel behavior.
evidence.subsection: Financial Overview

evidence.text: Aircraft fuel and related taxes increase d 12.7 % primarily due to a 13.4 % increase in the average price per gallon of fuel to $1.66 in the third quarter of 2017 from $1.46 in the 2016 period. Interest income increase d $28 million principally due to an increase in interest rates, which drove more than a 50 basis point increase in average yields in the third quarter of 2017 as compared to the 2016 period.
evidence.subsection: Three Months Ended September 30, 2017 Compared to Three Months Ended September 30, 2016

evidence.text: Our business, results of operations and financial condition have been and will continue to be affected by many changing economic and other conditions beyond our control, including global events that affect travel behavior, and our results of operations could be volatile and fluctuate due to seasonality.
evidence.subsection: We depend on a limited number of suppliers for aircraft, aircraft engines and parts.

evidence.text: In particular, an outbreak of a contagious disease such as the Ebola virus, Middle East Respiratory Syndrome, Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome, H1N1 influenza virus, avian flu, Zika virus or any other similar illness, if it were to become associated with air travel or persist for an extended period, could materially affect the airline industry and us by reducing revenues and adversely impacting our operations and passengers’ travel behavior.
evidence.subsection: We depend on a limited number of suppliers for aircraft, aircraft engines and parts.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: The company is highly sensitive to external factors like fuel costs, economic conditions, and global events, which pose risks to its performance.
